interactions.summary: Jiang presses students on scarcity after arguing money is infinite; several student replies show they remain inside the scarcity frame until one says those who have money do not want to share.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang with short student replies in seg-0026, seg-0028, and seg-0030.

interactions.summary: Jiang turns the student's answer into a model: poverty, crises, and money destruction are imposed by the powerful to preserve the illusion that money is valuable and keep people working.

speaker_notes.note: Some student answers are fragmentary or mis-diarized, but they function as short attempts to answer Jiang's poverty question.

claims.claim: Jiang distinguishes physical limitation from social scarcity: money is not scarce because it is only a number that can be printed in unlimited quantity.
Poverty exists, in Jiang's model, because powerful people need artificial misery to make money feel valuable and to make work feel necessary.
Economic crises are presented as mechanisms for destroying money so that people continue to experience money as scarce.

signature_moments.moment: The students keep believing money is scarce right after being told it can be printed infinitely.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The scene demonstrates Jiang's argument about power: an installed idea survives direct contradiction.

signature_moments.moment: Poverty is not self-inflicted failure; it is what power does to people so money looks real.
signature_moments.source_phrase: poverty isn't what you do to yourself. It is what the powerful do to you
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the packet's core reversal and one of the lecture's sharpest public theses.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The internalized belief that money and resources are naturally scarce, even when Jiang argues the monetary system can create abundance.
Jiang's description of poverty as manufactured suffering that motivates people to work for money.
